notes.anchor: the fifth day before the Ides of May
notes.note: The Ides of May fell on the 15th; counted inclusively, the fifth day before is 11 May 51 BC, when Cicero reached Beneventum.

A scriba was a professional public clerk who handled magistrates' records and accounts. This M. Tullius (no relation to Cicero) served as Cicero's secretary and kept the accounts of his Cilician proconsulship.

notes.anchor: if a decree of the Senate is passed
notes.note: A senatus consultum was a formal resolution of the Senate. Cicero hopes for one authorising reimbursement of his and Bibulus's outfitting expenses, but notes that even without it Atticus can secure the payment.

notes.anchor: especially when there is in it a saving for the people
notes.note: A measure that saved the public treasury (a compendium populi) money was easy to carry; Cicero expects little opposition to a decree that costs the state nothing extra.

notes.anchor: Do not lay it before the Senate, consul!
notes.note: Only a presiding magistrate (here a consul) could put a matter to the Senate (referre ad senatum). Cicero jokingly mimics the obstruction a friend would need only to deflect, not actively manage, the trivial business referred to him.
The cry numera! ("count!") demanded a head-count to show the Senate lacked a quorum, a standard delaying tactic to prevent a vote.
The Kalends were the first day of the month, so this means before 1 June 51 BC, the deadline by which the legate Pomptinus needed to reach Brundisium for the crossing east.

Praefecti were officers a proconsul appointed to his staff (cohors); the prefectures of a governor's entourage were sought-after favours, and Cicero awaited Pompey's wishes about five such appointments.

notes.anchor: the matter of the eight hundred thousand sesterces
notes.note: The 800,000 sesterces (HS) of credit opened through Oppius (cf. 5.1); Cicero presses Atticus to settle the account before leaving for Epirus.
